title: Biochemistry, Water Soluble Vitamins  -- Introduction
content: Vitamins play a vital role in many biochemical functions in the human body and are essential components for maintaining optimal health. There are two main groups of vitamins – fat-soluble (easily stored in fat upon absorption) and water-soluble (washed out and not easily stored). Although adequate intake of all vitamins is important, regular intake is required to avoid deficiency due to the transient nature of water-soluble vitamins. The water-soluble vitamins include Vitamin C and Vitamin B complex (thiamine, riboflavin, niacin, pantothenic acid, pyridoxine, biotin, folate, and cobalamin).

title: Riboflavin Deficiency -- Introduction
content: Riboflavin, vitamin B2, is a water-soluble and heat-stable vitamin that the body uses to metabolize fats, protein, and carbohydrates into glucose for energy. In addition to boosting energy, riboflavin is an antioxidant for the proper immune system function and healthy skin and hair. These effects occur with the help of two coenzymes, flavin mononucleotide (FMN) and flavin adenine dinucleotide (FAD). Without an adequate amount of riboflavin, macronutrients like carbohydrates, fats, and proteins cannot be digested and maintained by the body. With a healthy digestive system, the body can absorb most of the nutrients from the diet, so it is important to get most of the riboflavin from dietary sources. Riboflavin has a yellow-green fluorescent pigment, which causes urine to turn yellow, indicating the body is absorbing riboflavin. Riboflavin also helps convert tryptophan to niacin, which activates vitamin B6. Some preventable diseases manageable with adequate riboflavin are anemia, cataracts, migraines, and thyroid dysfunction. Riboflavin is necessary for normal development, lactation, physical performance, and reproduction. [1] [2]

title: Vitamin B2 (Riboflavin) -- Indications
content: Milk and dairy products are rich in riboflavin and vitamin D. Dairy consumption is the primary source of vitamin D in Western diets. This is why riboflavin deficiency is rare among water-soluble vitamins. However, an increased intake of semi-skimmed milk in developed countries depletes milk's riboflavin content. Although relatively stable, it easily degrades with light exposure. Milk in a glass bottle may contain less riboflavin if exposed to light.

title: Biochemistry, Water Soluble Vitamins  -- Clinical Significance
content: Vitamins are classified into two categories based on how they are absorbed and if they are stored. Water-soluble vitamins dissolve in water upon entering the body. Because of this, humans cannot store excess amounts of water-soluble vitamins for later use. There are nine water-soluble vitamins: the B vitamins -- folate, thiamine, riboflavin, niacin, pantothenic acid, biotin, vitamin B6, and vitamin B12 -- and vitamin C. Deficiency of any of these water-soluble vitamins results in a clinical syndrome that may result in severe morbidity and mortality.

title: Riboflavin Deficiency -- Pathophysiology
content: Research has shown that riboflavin deficiency can alter iron absorption and cause anemia, which leads to fatigue. Riboflavin is involved in red blood cell production and transportation of oxygen to the cells. Improving the amount of riboflavin in the body can increase circulating hemoglobin levels and increase red cell production. Collagen is a protein found in most skin and hair, so riboflavin is necessary to maintain an adequate collagen level. Taking supplements of riboflavin is also a cure for migraines. Research showed that 400 mg of riboflavin a day had demonstrated efficacy in the prevention of migraine headaches in adults, but dosing must be for a minimum of 3 months for good results. This finding is most likely because mitochondrial dysfunction has been shown to play a role in migraines, and riboflavin is a precursor of flavin cofactors of the electron transport chain. According to research, riboflavin supplements can help with mitochondrial complex I deficiency by improving muscle strength, cardiomyopathy, and encephalopathy. A cataract is an eye disorder causing blurry lenses, and taking riboflavin supplements can help prevent cataracts.

title: Vitamin B2 (Riboflavin) -- Indications
content: Grain products possess low natural amounts, but fortification practices ensure that certain bread and cereals have become sources of riboflavin. Therefore, according to an article by Morgan KJ et al, high riboflavin levels were found in those who had cereals for breakfast. [2] Fatty fish are also excellent sources of riboflavin, and certain fruits and vegetables, especially dark green vegetables, contain reasonably high concentrations. Vegetarians with access to various fruits and vegetables can avoid deficiency, although intake may be lower than omnivores, and elderly vegetarians are at a higher risk. [3]

title: Vitamin B2 (Riboflavin) -- Indications
content: Riboflavin, also known as vitamin B2, is a water-soluble vitamin that belongs to the vitamin B complex group. Clinicians frequently prescribe riboflavin as part of a combined formulation with other B complex vitamins as a prophylactic supplement to manage and treat vitamin B2 deficiency. Riboflavin deficiency is a rare condition as it is present in various food choices. However, individuals who follow a diet low in meat and milk, which are considered the best sources of riboflavin, and certain specific groups of people, as discussed below, may be more susceptible to this deficiency. [1]

title: Physiology, Nutrient Absorption -- Pathophysiology -- Fat Absorption
content: Water-soluble vitamins include thiamine (B1), riboflavin (B2), niacin (B3), pantothenic acid (B5), pyridoxine (B6), biotin (B7), folic acid (B9), cobalamin (B12), and ascorbic acid (C). [49] Water-soluble vitamins do not simply traverse cellular membranes but are absorbed in the digestive tract through specific carrier-mediated pathways. After distribution, they are consumed by the tissues for their metabolic needs.

title: Vitamin B2 (Riboflavin) -- Mechanism of Action
content: Riboflavin plays a principal role in the electron transport chain. [15] Inadequate riboflavin intake would be expected to disturb the intermediate steps of metabolism, with specific functional implications. The conversion of the amino acid tryptophan to niacin requires FAD. Similarly, converting vitamin B6 (pyridoxine) to the coenzyme pyridoxal 5’-phosphate needs FMN. [16]

title: Vitamin B2 (Riboflavin) -- Mechanism of Action -- Pharmacokinetics
content: Absorption: A small amount of riboflavin is present in foods as free riboflavin, a majority as its derivative flavin adenine dinucleotide (FAD), and a smaller amount as flavin mononucleotide (FMN). Intestinal bacteria also produce a small amount of riboflavin. [18] For the absorption of dietary riboflavin, a prerequisite is converting FAD and FMN to free riboflavin, catalyzed by enzymes called phosphatases in the enterocyte. [19] Absorption occurs predominantly in the small intestine through a carrier-mediated transport process by RFVT3 (riboflavin transporter). [20] The absorption of riboflavin is increased by food.

title: UMLS -- Run 26 -- Level 2 -- Community 597
content: Iron‑containing multivitamins are designed to supply a broad spectrum of essential nutrients in a single dosage form, often for patients with increased micronutrient needs such as those who have undergone bariatric or malabsorptive surgery. The iron component is provided as polysaccharide iron complex, ferrous asparto‑glycinate, iron sucrose, or ferric pyrophosphate, frequently combined with a full vitamin B complex (thiamine, riboflavin, niacinamide, pyridoxine, cyanocobalamin B12, and folic acid). Vitamin C is added to enhance iron absorption, while vitamin A, vitamin D, vitamin E (α‑tocopherol acetate), beta‑carotene and DHA support visual, skeletal and neurological health. Mineral supplementation includes calcium (as calcium carbonate, calcium ascorbate or calcium threonate), magnesium, zinc, copper and iodine to counteract postoperative deficiencies. Additional excipients such as succinic acid, docusate, essential fatty acids and probiotics are incorporated to improve

title: UMLS -- Run 21 -- Level 2 -- Community 838
content: The IRON/MULTIVITAMINS complex is a parent category that includes a broad range of iron‑containing multivitamin products such as BARIATRIC MULTIVITAMINS W/IRON TAB CHEWABLE, Multi‑Day with Calcium and Extra Iron, MULTIVITAMINS W/IRON DROPS ORAL, VITAMIN B COMPLEX W/C & IRON CAP, VITAMIN B COMPLEX W/C & IRON TAB, VITAMIN B COMPLEX W/C, IRON, ZINC TAB, and VITAMIN B/FERRIC PYROPHOSPHATE 100 MG/FA 0.25 MG LIQUID. These formulations combine iron (as ferrous asparto‑glycinate, polysaccharide iron complex, ferrous sulfate, ferric pyrophosphate) with a spectrum of micronutrients—including calcium ascorbate, calcium threonate, folic acid, succinic acid, vitamin B12, vitamins C, A (cholecalciferol), D, zinc, magnesium, copper, vitamin E (alpha‑tocopherol acetate), essential fatty acids, probiotics, docusate, and the full B‑vitamin complex (thiamine, riboflavin, niacinamide, pyridoxine, cyanocobalamin). The products are offered in multiple dosage forms—oral capsules, tablets, extended‑release tablets, chewable tablets, and liquid preparations—to accommodate diverse patient needs, particularly individuals who have undergone bariatric surgery or who require combined iron and micronutrient supplementation. Folic acid, a key component of these products, supports normal hematopoiesis, enhances iron absorption, and prevents nutritional deficiencies that can cause anemia; its deficiency is directly associated with macrocytic (megaloblastic) anemia. The integrated strategy of the IRON/MULTIVITAMINS family therefore addresses concurrent iron deficiency and broader vitamin/mineral insufficiencies, providing patient‑friendly regimens for preventive health care and the management of anemia‑related disorders.

Vitamin classifications follow ISA hierarchies: “Vitamin A, plain” and “Vitamin D and analogues” belong to “vitamin A and D, incl. combinations”; “Vitamin B₁, plain” is ISA to “vitamin B₁, plain and in combination with vitamin B₆ and B₁₂”; within the “Vitamin B₁₂ (cyanocobalamin and analogues)” family, mecobalamin and hydroxokobalamin are noted alternatives. Vitamin C entities follow a similar structure, with “ascorbic acid (vitamin C), incl. combinations” ISA to both “vitamin C combinations” and “vitamin C products, plain,” and the specific combination “ascorbic acid and calcium” illustrating the frequent pairing of calcium to enhance absorption. title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1863
content: IRON/MULTIVITAMINS is a multivitamin formulation that incorporates iron and a broad spectrum of vitamins, minerals, and other nutrients to support overall health. The product is offered in several formats, including a Multi‑Day pack that adds calcium and extra iron, as well as oral capsule preparations marketed as Multivitamins w/ Iron. Its active ingredients are detailed in multiple ingredient listings: a vitamin B complex combined with vitamin C, folic acid, and iron (available as both tablets and capsules); a vitamin B complex plus vitamin C, folic acid, iron, and probiotics in capsule form; a vitamin B complex with folic acid, iron, and essential fatty acids in capsules; vitamin B12 paired with vitamin C, folic acid, and iron in capsules; vitamin B12 with folic acid and iron in capsules; and a liquid preparation that blends vitamins A, D, and C with iron. Additionally, a specific liquid ingredient, VITAMIN B/FERRIC PYROPHOSPHATE 100 MG/FA 0.25 MG, is included, providing a bioavailable source of iron. Classified as a “multivitamin agent,” IRON/MULTIVITAMINS serves as the active ingredient in these various formulations, delivering iron alongside nutrients that support hematopoiesis (folic acid, vitamin B12), antioxidant protection (vitamin C), bone health (calcium, vitamin D), and cellular function (essential fatty acids and probiotics). This integrated composition is intended to address iron deficiency while simultaneously supplying complementary vitamins and minerals essential for comprehensive patient care.

title: UMLS -- Run 19 -- Level 3 -- Community 2272
content: IRON/MULTIVITAMINS is classified as a multivitamin‑plus‑mineral preparation and encompasses a broad range of formulations that combine iron with various vitamins and nutrients. The product line includes specific items such as “MULTIVITAMINS W/IRON DROPS, ORAL” and “VITAMIN B COMPLEX W/C & IRON CAP,” each of which contain iron together with vitamin B complexes, vitamin C, folic acid, and vitamin B12 in oral dosage forms. Additional formulations are described as “multivitamin with iron Vitamin B Complex with C, Folic Acid and Iron oral capsule,” “multivitamin with iron Vitamin B Complex with C, Folic Acid and Iron oral tablet,” and “multivitamin with iron Vitamin B Complex with Folic Acid, Iron, and Essential Fatty Acids oral capsule,” highlighting the inclusion of essential fatty acids alongside the vitamin‑iron blend. Other variants emphasize vitamin A, D, and C combined with iron in an oral liquid, and a calcium‑enriched version labeled “Multi‑Day with Calcium and Extra Iron,” which represents a narrower, calcium‑focused subcategory within the broader IRON/MULTIVATIMINS family. Across these products, the common therapeutic intent is to provide supplemental iron together with a spectrum of B‑group vitamins, vitamin C, folic acid, vitamin B12, and additional micronutrients (such as essential fatty acids, calcium, vitamin A, and vitamin D) in convenient oral forms—including capsules, tablets (including extended‑release), drops, and liquids—to support iron repletion and overall nutritional status.

Such products are used to prevent nutritional deficiency, support pregnancy, and provide comprehensive micronutrient repletion. Antianemic preparations form a therapeutic subclass that specifically pairs vitamin B12 (cyanocobalamin, mecobalamin, hydroxokobalamin) with folic acid to treat megaloblastic (macrocytic) anemia. They are distinguished from iron‑based antianemic agents, which rely on iron as the primary active ingredient. An oral tablet “folic acid 1 mg / vitamin B12 0.5 mg” exemplifies the vitamin B12‑and‑folic‑acid complex. title: UMLS -- Run 22 -- Level 1 -- Community 62
content: Vitamins constitute a broad therapeutic class that includes individual agents such as hydroxokobalamin (vitamin B12), cyanocobalamin, mecobalamin, folic acid (kyselina listová), vitamin A (plain and acetate), vitamin B1 (thiamine), vitamin B6 (pyridoxine), vitamin B12 (cobalamin family), vitamin C (ascorbic acid), vitamin D (cholecalciferol), vitamin E (α‑tocopherol acetate), and vitamin K₃, as well as combined forms (e.g., vitamin A + D, vitamin B‑complex). Multivitamin‑plus‑mineral preparations integrate these vitamins with essential minerals (calcium carbonate, ferrous fumarate, zinc oxide, copper oxide, magnesium‑L‑threonate, phosphorus, copper, zinc, calcium ascorbate, calcium threonate) and bioactive compounds (essential fatty acids, probiotics, NADH, flavin adenine dinucleotide, phosphatidylserine, betaine, succinic acid). The inclusion of folic acid alongside iron and other nutrients is intended to support hematopoiesis, improve red‑cell production, and address nutritional deficiencies. Clinically, folic acid is directly linked to the prevention and treatment of macrocytic anemia, known in Arabic as فقر دم ضَخم الأرومات. title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 3 -- Community 1502
content: Pantothenát vápenatý, the calcium salt of pantothenic acid (vitamin B5), is classified as a calcium compound and belongs to the vitamin B complex family. It serves as a source of vitamin B5 in a wide range of multivitamin and mineral preparations, including oral capsules that also contain iron, as well as numerous branded multivitamin tablets and liquid‑filled capsules that combine it with other vitamins (such as vitamin A, vitamin C, vitamin D, thiamine, riboflavin, niacin, pyridoxine, folic acid, cyanocobalamin, biotin, calcium carbonate, magnesium, zinc, copper, and omega‑3 fatty acids). In most of these formulations pantothenát vápenatý is listed as an active ingredient, although in at least one powder supplement it appears among the inactive components, reflecting variability in labeling practices. Through its calcium moiety, pantothenát vápenatý exhibits phosphate‑chelating activity, which can influence mineral metabolism and contribute to the bioavailability of other nutrients. Because of its calcium content, the compound is contraindicated in individuals with conditions that predispose to calcium overload, specifically kidney stones (حصاة في الكلية) and hypercalcemia (فرط كالسيوم الدم). Awareness of these contraindications is important when prescribing or recommending supplements containing pantothenát vápenatý to ensure patient safety while providing the nutritional benefits of vitamin B5.

Iron is supplied through polysaccharide iron complex, ferrous asparto‑glycinate, iron sucrose, ferric pyrophosphate and sodium feredetate, providing elemental iron while enhancing gastrointestinal tolerability. Calcium is provided as calcium ascorbate, calcium threonate or calcium carbonate and is frequently combined with vitamin D (cholecalciferol) to support bone health. The vitamin profile consistently includes vitamin A (β‑carotene), vitamin D, vitamin E (α‑tocopherol acetate), vitamin K (when present), the full B‑vitamin complex (thiamine, riboflavin, niacinamide, pyridoxine, cyanocobalamin, vitamin B12), and vitamin C (ascorbic acid).
